ocr.text: The provision of Emergency Obstetric and Newborn Care (EmONC) services is critical for improving maternal and neonatal
health outcomes, particularly in regions with limited healthcare resources. The data table presented highlights the varying
percentages of healthcare facilities that provide both Basic and Comprehensive EmONC services, specifically focusing on the
administration of parenteral antibiotics and the execution of signal functions 1–7. For instance, the reported percentages of
facilities in McGill and Birmingham demonstrate notable differences in the availability of these essential services, with McGill
reporting 37.5% (n=27) for Basic EmONC services as compared to Birmingham's 43.86% (n=75). Furthermore, the statistical
analysis, indicated by the χ2 and p value metrics, serves to assess the significance of these differences, offering insights into the
accessibility and quality of obstetric care in these two distinct locations.
Basic EmONC services  Comprehensive EmONC services
1) Administer parenteral antibiotics  Perform signal functions 1–7, plus
2) Administer uterotonic drugs  8) Perform surgery (cesarean section)
3) Administer parenteral anticonvulsants  9) Perform blood transfusion
4) Manually remove the placenta
5) Remove retained products
6) Perform assisted vaginal delivery
7) Perform basic neonatal resuscitation
Understanding these disparities is crucial for policymakers and healthcare practitioners seeking to enhance service delivery,
ensuring that all women have access to the necessary care during pregnancy and childbirth.
Options  McGill % (n)  Birmingham % (n)  χ2, p value  Non-medical staff % (n)  χ2, p value
Such data not only underscores the importance of comprehensive service provision but also highlights areas where further
investment and focus are needed to improve health outcomes.
